title: Elements of Drama Warm-Up
walt: Identify and use the elements of drama through online drama games.
success: I can name at least three elements of drama.
I can use two elements of drama in a short improvisation.
wp: who you are pretending to be
where you are and what is happening
brain: Which element makes a freeze frame feel dramatic?
How can voice alone change the mood of one line?
Why does drama need contrast?

teach.title: Mini-lesson — Elements (App. A)
teach.body: Recap the 10 elements of drama: role, situation, voice, movement, space, time, tension, mood, focus, contrast.
teach.tip: Share App. A on screen. Keep it tight and clearly labelled.

teach.title: Re-teach with a student example
teach.body: Spotlight one volunteer. Name the elements you see. Tighten the use of voice and tension together.
teach.tip: Keep demos under two minutes so students get maximum doing time.

sequence: Call a word (celebration, storm, market) — students freeze in a pose that shows it. Gallery view; spotlight 3–4 each round.
Recap App. A — role, situation, voice, movement, space, time, tension, mood, focus, contrast. Share on screen; keep labels clear.
On mute, cameras on: 30–45 sec moment (mysterious box / waiting for big news) using at least two named elements. Drop situations in chat. No breakouts.
Spotlight one volunteer; name elements; tighten voice and tension.
Spotlight performances one at a time; class names elements in chat. Mute other mics.
Recap elements + exit ticket. Preview Lesson 2 — a short history of drama.
model: Perform a 20–30 sec mini-scene live, naming the two elements as you go: “watch for voice and tension here.”
watch: Drama warm-up & improv games · National Theatre / BBC Teach (~3 min)
exit: A. Phone call with fantastic news — react and reply
B. Mystery package at the door — open it on camera
C. Winning a strange competition — thank-you speech
resources: Webcam + mic · App. A on screen · element list pinned in chat
notes:
Appendix A on screen. Camera Freeze → elements mini-lesson → solo build → re-teach voice/tension → spotlight shares → 30-sec exit ticket.

Whole-class week — no breakouts yet.

title: A Short History of Drama
walt: Understand how drama has changed across time, and what a monologue and a soliloquy are.
success: I can describe one feature of drama from at least two time periods.
I can explain the difference between a monologue and a soliloquy.
wp: one character speaks to someone or the audience
private thoughts spoken aloud, usually alone
group that helped tell the story in Greek theatre
brain: Why did Greek actors wear masks?
How is a soliloquy different from chatting to a friend on stage?
What stayed the same from Greek theatre to filming at home?

teach.title: Timeline, part 1 (App. B)
teach.body: Ancient Greek theatre & medieval mystery/morality plays — two or three key facts each.
teach.tip: Keep each era memorable, not exhaustive. Share App. B on screen.

teach.title: Timeline, part 2 + mono vs soliloquy
teach.body: Elizabethan/Shakespearean & modern/digital drama (App. B). Introduce monologue vs soliloquy with App. C.
teach.tip: Act the same line both ways so the difference is obvious.

sequence: Call an era — masked Greek actor, medieval town-square player, Shakespearean actor, modern film star — students pose. Ask 2–3 to explain a choice.
App. B: Ancient Greek & medieval — 2–3 key facts each.
Pose or mime one feature per era so far; drop one fact in chat. Read a few aloud. No breakouts.
Elizabethan/Shakespearean & modern/digital + monologue vs soliloquy (App. C).
Independently jot 2–3 character ideas for a later monologue or soliloquy. Spotlight a few — mono or soliloquy? Quiet thinking first.
Recap mono vs soliloquy + exit ticket. Preview Lesson 3 — drama across cultures (first breakouts!).
model: Say one line twice: first as a monologue (to someone), then as a soliloquy (private thoughts, alone). Ask the class to spot the difference.
watch: Shakespeare’s Theatre (the Globe) · BBC Teach (~4 min) · youtube.com/watch?v=D1rbtHchv1g
exit: A. Masked Greek actor announces huge news to the city
B. Soliloquy: alone, deciding something big
C. Shakespearean town crier announces tomorrow’s event
resources: App. B History Timeline · App. C Monologue vs Soliloquy (on screen)
notes: Appendices B & C. Act the same line both ways. Character seeds feed Weeks 3–10 — no single right answer yet.

title: Drama Across Cultures
walt: Describe how drama communicates ideas and helps continue and revitalise culture.
success: I can describe one way a performance communicated meaning.
I can explain why storytelling matters to a culture.
wp: shared beliefs, stories and ways of life
a story passed down through generations
clever character who outwits a stronger foe
bring new life and energy to something
brain: What feelings did the performance communicate?
Why might this story matter to its culture?
Which story pattern could inspire your own piece?

teach.title: Teach + view storytelling clip
teach.body: Play the chosen storytelling/drama clip(s). Pause to ask: what is happening, and what ideas or feelings are being communicated?
teach.tip: Share screen with audio. Pre-test the link; have a backup clip ready. Preview for cultural appropriateness.

teach.title: Character & situation (App. D)
teach.body: Use App. D patterns to choose a character + situation — from a culture, folktale, history, or an original idea. Invite stories from students’ own backgrounds.
teach.tip: First breakouts this week — pin two questions in each room; clear time limit + 2-min warning.

sequence: One-sentence prompt; volunteers add a sentence (aloud or chat) to build a class story. Read chat additions aloud.
Play storytelling clip; pause for meaning and feeling.
Think-pair-share: what did it communicate, and why might the story matter to its culture? Pin both questions.
Patterns for character + situation; invite own-culture inspiration.
Independently choose character + situation; note where inspiration came from. Follow up anyone still undecided.
Exit ticket. Character + situation idea due at the start of Lesson 4.
model: Model answering “what did this communicate?” aloud, citing voice/movement/situation — then show how App. D helps lock a character + situation.
watch: Storytelling traditions around the world · TED-Ed / ABC Education (teacher-selected 1–2 clips)
exit: A. Opening of a folktale from your family or culture
B. Wise elder passing on one important lesson
C. Hero discovers something amazing
resources: 1–2 vetted culture clips · App. D Character Shortlist · breakout rooms (pairs)
notes: Appendix D. Breakout debut — pre-assign rooms, pin questions, pop into a couple of rooms to settle the routine.

title: Building Your Character
walt: Build a character through role, situation and voice.
success: I can describe my character’s role and situation.
I can find a voice and posture for my character.
wp: answering questions in role as your character
who you are pretending to be
how your body shows the character
brain: What does your character want most right now?
How would they walk into a room?
Which App. F question reveals the most?

teach.title: Hot-seating demo (App. F)
teach.body: Recap role, situation, voice. Demonstrate hot-seating — answer in role for about a minute using an App. F prompt.
teach.tip: Ask a volunteer to interview you in role so pairs see the format.

teach.title: Into the planning template (App. E)
teach.body: Show how to turn hot-seat answers into the Monologue Planning Template — character, situation and voice.
teach.tip: Keep a copy of templates so work isn’t lost if a student drops out.

sequence: Move on camera as characters you call — queen, trickster, giant, animal. Discuss how movement shows role.
Answer in role ~1 min with App. F; volunteer interviews you.
Pairs take turns hot-seating each other’s character with App. F prompts. Rotate rooms so both get a turn.
Transfer answers onto App. E — character, situation, voice.
Independently finalise character, situation and voice on the planning template.
Share character in one sentence + exit ticket. Preview Lesson 5 — finding the key moment.
model: Volunteer asks you one question; answer in role for ~30 sec with committed voice and posture.
watch: Hot-seating: building a character · BBC Bitesize / Drama Resource (~3 min)
exit: A. Return a faulty product — explain the problem in character
B. Voicemail explaining why you’re late
C. Talk-show intro — name, age, biggest want
resources: Breakout pairs · App. E Planning Template · App. F Hot-Seat prompts
notes: Appendix F hot-seat then App. E template. Model in role before pairs swap.

title: Finding Your Moment — Structure & Tension
walt: Combine elements of drama to shape and sustain a one-minute piece.
success: I can choose a key dramatic moment for my piece.
I can plan how tension builds across my piece.
brain: Where is the peak of your story?
What happens if you pause before the big line?
How can stillness create tension?

teach.title: One-minute structure (App. G)
teach.body: Opening line → build → turning point → final line. Show how tension, focus and contrast create interest across the minute.
teach.tip: Leave App. G on screen as a visual prompt students can refer back to.

teach.title: Pause + contrast
teach.body: Model how a pause and a contrast turn an ordinary line into the peak moment.
teach.tip: Same words twice: flat, then pause + contrast — difference in 30 sec.

sequence: Pairs create before/after freezes either side of a tense moment; partners guess what happened. Bring 2–3 back to show.
App. G: opening, build, turning point, final line + tension/focus/contrast.
Pairs: before/after tableau for each student’s own turning point; partner guesses the moment.
Model flat line vs paused/contrasted peak.
On App. E: opening line, how it builds, turning point (highest tension), final line. One suggestion per student.
Share a planned opening or final line + exit ticket. Preview Lesson 6 — devising words and voice.
model: Deliver one line flat, then again with a pause + contrast (loud → silent). Same words, very different tension.
watch: Building tension & using tableaux · Drama Resource / BBC Bitesize (~3 min)
exit: A. 30 seconds before opening a mysterious box
B. Countdown — narrate 30 sec before something huge
C. Waiting for news that could change everything
resources: Breakout pairs · App. E (continued) · App. G Structure Prompt Card
notes: Appendix G. Slow the turning point; use contrast and pause. Check in by room with one tip each.

title: Devising the Words & Voice
walt: Use improvisation to devise the words and voice for our piece.
success: I can improvise to find lines for my monologue or soliloquy.
I can use voice to show my character’s feelings.
wp: the feeling colour in your voice
how fast or slow you speak
how loud or soft you speak
brain: Which line would you keep from that improv?
How does pace change meaning?
Where should the voice match the turning point?

teach.title: Devising techniques (App. H)
teach.body: Solo devising: talk it out, partner thought-tracking, record & listen back. Demonstrate one live with a volunteer.
teach.tip: Keep the demo under two minutes — protect work time.

teach.title: Voice toolkit
teach.body: Tone, pace and volume show feeling — match the voice to the turning point.
teach.tip: Link voice choices explicitly to the App. G peak.

sequence: Same line — “Wait, look over there!” — in three voices/emotions. Quick whole-class unmuted round.
App. H techniques; demo one with a volunteer (<2 min).
Find and lock lines with a partner prompting/listening; note lines on App. E. Suggest a technique if stuck.
Solo: run start to finish once (even roughly) with voice choices applied.
Share one proud line + exit ticket. Preview rehearsal lessons.
model: “Talk it out” live for ~30 sec in role, then say which line you’d keep — modelling how devising works.
watch: Using your voice as an actor · National Theatre (~4 min)
exit: A. “We need to talk” three ways: scared, excited, furious
B. 30-sec rant in character about a tiny annoyance
C. Same line, two emotions — show the switch on camera
resources: Breakout pairs · App. H Devising Techniques · App. E Planning Template
notes: Improvisation to find words and voice. Keep demos under two minutes. Piece should hang together end to end by the end of the lesson.

title: Performance Skills for the Camera
walt: Use performance skills suited to performing on camera.
success: I can project my voice clearly through the microphone.
I can use facial expression and the camera frame effectively.
wp: how much of you appears in the shot
where your eyes look on camera
small, clear moves that read on screen
brain: What ruins an otherwise great performance on camera?
Why does looking at the lens matter?
What one tip would you give a peer?

teach.title: On-camera skills
teach.body: Voice projection vs mic distance; facial expression close to camera; small clear gestures in frame; check background and lighting.
teach.tip: Model a less-effective then more-effective example side by side.

teach.title: Peer-tip protocol
teach.body: How to give one quick, specific tip on framing or voice — kind and concrete.
teach.tip: Ban vague “be better” — require one concrete action.

sequence: Check how much body is visible; tips on sitting/standing back, lighting, mic. Share 1–2 good framing examples.
Projection, expression, gestures, frame, light — less vs more effective model.
Several full runs using App. I checklist. On mute, cameras on; spotlights for quick tips.
Protocol for one kind, concrete tip.
Partners watch one run each; one tip on framing or voice.
Share one improvement + exit ticket. Action at least one feedback point before Lesson 8.
model: Same few lines twice: too far/flat/glancing away → good frame, eyes to lens, clear voice.
watch: Acting for camera — tips · StageMilk / BBC Bitesize (~4 min)
exit: A. Strongest 30 sec to camera — eyes to the lens
B. 30-sec news report in character
C. Close-up emotion change using just your face
resources: Breakout pairs · App. I Rehearsal Checklist
notes: Model less-effective then more-effective side by side. Circulate via spotlight during solo rehearsals.

title: Rehearsal & Peer Feedback
walt: Refine our performance using peer feedback and explain how we used elements of drama.
success: I can give a helpful comment using drama vocabulary.
I can explain one element of drama used in my piece.
wp: specific, kind comments that help refine
one of the 10 drama building blocks
what the choice makes the audience feel
improve a small part with intention
brain: What makes feedback useful?
Which element did you use on purpose?
What will you change after today?

teach.title: Feedback language (App. J)
teach.body: Recap specific, kind feedback using elements-of-drama vocabulary and App. J sentence starters.
teach.tip: Model vague “good job” vs specific element feedback so the difference is clear.

teach.title: Explain your elements
teach.body: How to say, in one sentence, which element you used and what effect it had — folio evidence language.
teach.tip: Bring App. A back on screen for vocabulary.

sequence: Vocal & physical warm-up + tongue-twister. Short and energetic before peer performing.
App. J + App. A — specific vs vague feedback.
Pairs perform; listener completes App. J sheet; swap; final adjustments. Check sheets are completed.
Write the explanation + one final tweak from feedback (folio evidence).
Volunteer shares feedback they’ll use + exit ticket. Pieces close to final before Week 9.
model: Two comments on the same performance: vague “good job” vs “the pause before your final line really built the tension.” Ask which helps more.
watch: A powerful short monologue — spot the elements · teacher-selected (~2 min)
exit: A. Re-record your turning point after today’s feedback
B. Director’s note to self — what you improved and why
C. Final line three different ways
resources: Breakout pairs · App. J Peer Feedback Sheet · App. A Elements reference
notes: Appendix A vocabulary again. Check feedback sheets are completed — this feeds the folio.

title: Independent Preparation
walt: Finalise and polish our piece, ready for filming.
success: I can perform my piece confidently from memory.
I know how I will film and submit my piece.
wp: send the finished film by the agreed method
brain: What still needs polishing?
Where will you film, and how will you light it?
What question will you bring to the help room?

teach.title: Goal + Filming Guide (App. K)
teach.body: Finalise and rehearse. Share App. K filming & submission guide: landscape, light, frame, quiet, one full take, watch back.
teach.tip: Pin App. K in the chat for the whole lesson.

teach.title: Whole-class readiness Q&A
teach.body: Show of hands on readiness; troubleshoot common tech questions (orientation, file size, upload) together.
teach.tip: Answer FAQs once for everyone to save independent time.

sequence: Goal + App. K on screen and pinned in chat.
Rehearse on camera; fine-tune lines/movement; optional help-room check-ins one at a time.
Confirm due date for Lesson 10 + exit ticket. Send a written reminder after class.
model: Walk App. K checklist aloud: landscape · face a window · upper body in frame · quiet · one full take · watch back.
watch: A student monologue performance · teacher-selected example (~2 min)
exit: A. Opening 30 sec — no script, one take
B. Confidence take in your actual filming spot
C. Best-framing test — strongest 30 sec, well lit
resources: App. I Rehearsal Checklist · App. K Filming & Submission Guide · help breakout
notes: Appendix K. Keep a help breakout open as a virtual queue (raise hand / chat). Weeks 9–10 are long DO by design.

title: Final Rehearsal, Filming & Reflection
walt: Perform our final piece with confidence and reflect on our drama journey.
success: I can perform my piece and sustain my character for one minute.
I can explain how I used elements of drama and what my piece communicates.
wp: keep character and focus for the full minute
think back on what you learned
who your piece is speaking to
brain: What are you most proud of?
Which element did the most work in your piece?
What would you try differently next time?

teach.title: Final filming tips
teach.body: Good light, quiet room, one full take with no stopping, watch the recording back before submitting. Reconfirm exact submission method and deadline.
teach.tip: Keep the App. K checklist on screen during the film block.

sequence: Individual vocal & physical warm-up on camera — stretches, breathing, tongue-twister. Guide whole-class to settle nerves.
Final rehearsals; film the one-minute piece; submit via agreed method. Help breakout open.
Whole-class return — how it felt (chat or aloud). Keep it light.
Reflection Sheet (App. L) for the folio — elements used + what the piece communicates. May finish as homework.
model: Reconfirm: quiet · landscape · light · upper body · one full take · watch back before submit.
watch: How to film yourself well at home — framing, light & sound (~3 min)
exit: A. Favourite 30 sec of your final piece
B. Reflection: the moment you’re proudest of
C. Director’s wrap speech — thank your “cast and crew”
resources: App. K · App. L Reflection Sheet · agreed submission method · help breakout
notes: Celebrate first — then App. L reflection for the folio. Filming checklist from App. K stays visible.
